narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and middle-family income background, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your plan to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated a 44.3% chance of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to prioritize students with stronger projected odds of bar passage. This prediction hinges on just a few key factors from your profile that carried the most weight in the model's assessment. The strongest influence pulling against you was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's 5.53 average; this low relative performance in your later undergraduate years signaled a notable risk of bar struggles, pushing the prediction toward failure more than any other element. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA stood out as a major strength, well above the average of 3.21, and it worked strongly in your favor by nudging the model toward expecting bar exam success. Your LSAT score of 29.50, however, which falls short of the dataset average of 36.70, also played a significant role, contributing toward the failure prediction despite your other academic highlights. Compared to typical students in the dataset, where full-time enrollment is the norm at over 92%, your status there didn't sway things much, but these core academic markers defined your case. While your GPA gives you a real edge over many peers, the combination of your lower Year 3 decile and LSAT created enough concern to outweigh it. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—models like this focus narrowly on bar passage patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, while reflecting on what might have impacted your Year 3 performance and addressing it through stronger references or a personal statement. Strengthening those areas could flip the model's outlook in a future application, opening doors at this or other programs. You're clearly capable, given your GPA, so targeted improvements now could make all the difference.
